what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard block item that records a design note titled "Where it bites regardless of option — the cross-cutting traps". It lists four system-design traps that apply to all options under discussion: Obsidian Sync racing with git, lossy projection of typed edges to wikilinks, cross-scheme addresses that have no vault file, and logical documents that do not map to folder structure.
observations: The item is typed as a block and linked part_of board://item-80c37615.
It frames Obsidian Sync as an adversarial second writer because it has its own conflict resolution and is unaware of board:// identity, typed edges, and mark-fold.
It argues git should be the canonical sync and Obsidian Sync should only run over a derived/projection vault, never the canonical directory.
It states the typed-edge to wikilink projection is lossy by design and must be one-way.
It notes that cross-scheme targets such as decision://, code://, and image:// have no markdown file in the vault, so Obsidian's graph cannot follow them natively.
It says the resolver, not Obsidian, is the only component that can actually follow a cross-scheme edge.
It describes a board document as a logical order list of block addresses with part_of links, stored flat and id-keyed, not foldered.
It mentions rendering a document via embedded/transcluded notes (![[block-id]]) while keeping sequence truth in the order field.
standouts: The adversarial-second-writer framing of Obsidian Sync.
The one-way, lossy typed-edge → wikilink rule.
The claim that the resolver, not Obsidian, is the only true cross-scheme edge follower.
conventions: Uses YAML front matter with id, address, type, source, state, title, links, and timestamps.
Uses a Markdown bullet list to enumerate design rationale and traps.
References cross-scheme address schemes such as board://, decision://, code://, and image://.
Uses section references like §1 law and mark-fold terminology without defining them here.
Breaks the common assumption that Obsidian Sync can be safely layered over a git-tracked canonical vault.
Breaks the naive bidirectional-sync assumption between typed edges and wikilinks.
concerns: Terms like "mark-fold", "§1 law", and "no-versioning law" are referenced but not defined in this file.
The shorthand options "A/C" and "D" are unexplained without external context.
The strategy for stub notes versus partial graph projection is left as an open decision.
The exact mechanism for generating and maintaining a derived/projection vault is not specified.
notes: A warning note arguing that Obsidian-native sync and wikilink graph views are lossy downstream lenses that must not write back to the canonical git-backed ledger.
questions: What are the design options A, C, and D being compared?
What is the "no-versioning law" and the "§1 law" referenced in the text?
How would a derived/projection vault be generated and kept in sync with the canonical directory?
What is the resolver's implementation and how does it resolve cross-scheme addresses?
What is the full schema for a board document's order list and part_of links?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose — documenting cross-cutting traps that apply regardless of option — matches the content, which lists four trap bullets about sync, edge projection, cross-scheme targets, and logical documents.
apparent_themes: Canonical source of truth versus lossy downstream projections
One-way projection instead of round-trip sync
Separation of logical document order from folder/file layout
Git as canonical sync and Obsidian Sync as downstream lens
Typed cross-scheme edges versus untyped wikilinks
intention_signals: It is trying to harden a design decision by enumerating non-obvious failure modes that cut across all architecture options, steering the team away from letting Obsidian Sync or wikilinks become write-back paths into the canonical ledger.
novelty: The explicit framing of a commercial sync feature as an "adversarial second writer" and the insistence on a one-way, lossy projection plane rather than bidirectional integration.
contribution: It contributes the invariant that git is the canonical sync, Obsidian Sync is only a downstream projection, typed edges must not be round-tripped through wikilinks, cross-scheme edges require a resolver, and document order is independent of folder structure.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard block item enumerating four cross-cutting design traps—Obsidian Sync as adversarial second writer, lossy typed-edge-to-wikilink projection, cross-scheme targets lacking files, and logical documents versus foldered storage—in a board/resolver-centric system.
intention_for_embedding: To warn that Obsidian-native sync and graph views are lossy downstream lenses that must not write back to or replace the canonical git-backed ledger.
